original_abstract:
This study provides a comprehensive cross-national analysis of the educational mobility of second-generation Turks in three distinct European contexts: Austria, France, and Sweden. Drawing on standardized data from the International Integration of the European Second Generation (TIES) survey, the research investigates why children of Turkish immigrants achieve varying levels of academic success across different national settings. The analysis moves beyond basic attainment metrics to explore "education pathways," tracing trajectories from pre-school through tertiary education to identify critical transition points where ethnic and social inequalities are produced or mitigated.

The findings reveal a significant "integration context" effect. Second-generation Turks in Sweden exhibit the highest levels of achievement and inter-generational mobility, followed by those in France, while those in Austria face the most constrained opportunities. Central to this variation is the interplay between individual-level resources—such as parental education, sibling support, and social capital from peers and teachers—and institutional arrangements. In highly stratified systems like Austria’s, characterized by early selection at age ten, educational success is heavily dependent on family resources. Conversely, the comprehensive, full-day systems in Sweden and France act as equalizers, reducing the dependency on domestic social capital and delaying high-stakes tracking. 

By analyzing local dynamics in five cities (Vienna, Linz, Paris, Strasbourg, and Stockholm), the study further identifies how sub-national variations in school segregation and teacher support influence outcomes. The research concludes that cross-national differences in mobility are primarily driven by the degree of institutional differentiation; late-selection systems with higher permeability between vocational and academic tracks provide the most favorable conditions for children of low-educated immigrants to successfully navigate the education system.
humanized_abstract:
This paper undertakes a cross-national comparative study of the educational trajectory of second generation Turks in three European countries: Austria, France and Sweden. Using data from the International Integration of the European Second Generation (TIES) project, the paper aims to explain the large variations in the educational outcomes of second generation Turks across countries. Moving beyond the straightforward focus on educational achievements, the paper studies the “education pathways” of second generation Turks from pre-school to higher education to better understand where, when and how social and ethnic inequalities materialise or are alleviated during the transition across different educational levels.

By highlighting the complex interplay of individual resources and institutional mechanisms that shape second-generation immigrants’ educational outcomes, the study contributes to a better understanding of the mechanisms of successful integration policies and the factors that explain cross-country differences in the level of social mobility achieved. Overall the study highlights the presence of a sizeable ‘integration context’ effect, with second-generation Turks in Sweden experiencing not only the highest level of educational achievement but also the largest level of intergenerational mobility compared to France and Austria. While parental education, the number of educated siblings and social capital from friends and teachers to some extent affect individual students’ achievements in all three countries, the overall impact of these resource factors and hence the degree of dependence of educational outcomes on the home environment is the highest in Austria and lowest in Sweden. The early selection at the age of ten in the Austrian education system is a clear contributing factor. The comprehensive full-day schooling found in Sweden and France works as a levelling mechanism reducing the effect of social capital in the home, and postpones the tracking that determines the future career perspectives of pupils, thereby reducing the dependence of students’ achievements on resources at home.

Building on insights gained from the investigation of local conditions in five urban case study settings (Vienna, Linz, Paris, Strasbourg and Stockholm), this report aims at exploring factors and mechanisms, present at the subnational level, that might explain possible disparities in the extent of school segregation and teachers’ support observed across different countries. In light of the research carried out, it seems that the bulk of cross-country differences in child mobility observed at the EU level can be attributed to the degree of institutional differentiation within each education system. Specifically, systems that have a late tracking point, and at the same time high transitional flexibility between different streams of secondary education tend to facilitate better educational trajectory change for children from low-educated immigrant background.
